assumption_registry.assumption: Trip contributions are support toward the trip rather than lending or equity.
evidence: GoFundMe validates public contribution behavior, but Crewly-specific social trip support intent remains untested.
assumption_registry.next_action: Run concept or landing page validation for public supporter intent.

assumption_registry.assumption: Organizer-controlled payout is acceptable for MVP if ledger transparency is clear.
evidence: User selected Option 3: public contribution checkout and organizer payout are delegated to an external crowdfunding/payment provider for MVP.
Crewly does not process, hold, refund, dispute, or pay out contribution funds directly in MVP.
assumption_registry.next_action: Stage 3 should model external provider links, contribution references, status import/reconciliation, and audit trails.

assumption_registry.assumption: Display-only currency conversion is sufficient for shared trip comprehension.
evidence: Stage 1 preserves original receipt values as source of truth and treats FX conversion as display-only.
assumption_registry.next_action: Carry source-versus-display labeling into Stage 3 data model and Stage 4 UX.

assumption_registry.assumption: Approved members can freely create private events without causing confusing or abusive subgroup sprawl.
evidence: Stage 1 creates clear participant-scoped private event visibility, but abuse and sprawl controls need Stage 3/4 design.
assumption_registry.next_action: Define event creation limits, audit logging, and reporting hooks downstream.

assumption_registry.assumption: Group trips are painful enough to justify a dedicated coordination hub.
evidence: Splitwise, Wanderlog, and GoFundMe independently validate demand around shared expenses, trip planning, and public fundraising.
assumption_registry.next_action: Validate Crewly's combined wedge with target users.

assumption_registry.assumption: Public supporters will contribute to selected social trips without private trip access.
evidence: GoFundMe validates public fundraiser contribution behavior; Crewly-specific contributor motivation remains unproven.
assumption_registry.next_action: Test public trip page clarity and contribution intent.

assumption_registry.assumption: Users will accept member-only media and organizer-controlled public highlights.
evidence: Stage 1 privacy boundary defaults media to private and requires explicit organizer promotion.
assumption_registry.next_action: Carry privacy defaults and moderation hooks into Stage 3 and Stage 4.

assumption_registry.assumption: Crewly can differentiate by combining public support, private coordination, receipts, and subgroup expense tracking.
evidence: Competitive review shows adjacent tools own separate components but not the exact Crewly combination.
assumption_registry.next_action: Keep the MVP scoped around the combined wedge.

assumption_registry.assumption: An external crowdfunding/payment provider can support public trip contributions and organizer payout outside Crewly's direct funds flow.
evidence: User selected Option 3: use an external crowdfunding/payment provider instead of Crewly-owned pooled payments for MVP.
External provider owns checkout, KYC, refunds, disputes, and payouts; Crewly stores contribution references/status only.
assumption_registry.next_action: Stage 5 must choose the specific provider and validate API/export/webhook support before implementation.

assumption_registry.assumption: Trip-wide ledgers and private event ledgers can remain separate without confusing users.
evidence: Stage 1 defines separate trip-scoped and event-scoped workflows and explicitly avoids event-to-trip rollup.
assumption_registry.next_action: Preserve separation in Stage 3 data and authorization design.

assumption_registry.assumption: Receipt OCR, translation, and display FX can be made trustworthy with manual correction and source preservation.
evidence: Manual correction and original source value preservation are part of the Stage 1 core receipt workflow.
assumption_registry.next_action: Model extraction confidence, correction audit trail, and source/display values in Stage 3.

assumption_registry.assumption: Moderation and reporting can contain public page and public highlight risk in MVP.
evidence: Moderation and reporting tooling is identified as a dependency, but workflows are not yet detailed.
assumption_registry.next_action: Define reporting, review, and audit hooks in Stage 3 and Stage 4.

assumption_registry.assumption: A viable monetization model exists for early Crewly usage.
evidence: No Stage 1 pricing model exists; adjacent competitors use different models.
assumption_registry.next_action: Validate organizer willingness to pay and payment fee tolerance.

assumption_registry.assumption: AI/document processing and storage costs can scale within early unit economics.
evidence: Receipt OCR, translation, media, and exchange-rate features are in MVP scope, but cost model is not yet defined.
assumption_registry.next_action: Estimate cost per trip, receipt, image, translation, and exchange-rate lookup.

market_validation.summary: Adjacent products validate the pain categories, but Crewly-specific contribution intent still needs user validation.

competitive_landscape.summary: Crewly is differentiated by combining public support, private trip coordination, receipt localization, and private event ledger separation.

user_validation.summary: Organizer and member pains are coherent from Stage 1, but public supporter contribution intent requires direct validation.

technical_validation.summary: Non-payment systems are feasible, and public contribution risk is mitigated by delegating checkout, KYC, refunds, disputes, and payouts to an external provider.

operational_validation.summary: Ledger separation, receipt source preservation, privacy defaults, audit logging, and moderation hooks must be preserved downstream.

business_validation.summary: Business wedge is plausible for Stage 3. MVP must not assume transaction margin from externally handled public contributions.

scalability_analysis.summary: Scalability is plausible if payment support load, media storage, receipt OCR, translation, and moderation costs are constrained and measured.

validated_product_model.public_surface: Selected trip page with external-provider contribution CTA, public updates, and optional organizer-promoted highlights.
validated_product_model.private_trip_surface: Approved-member workspace for details, ledger context, receipt records, member album, and trip updates.
validated_product_model.private_event_surface: Participant-scoped subgroup workspace with event expense and payment-status tracking only.
validated_product_model.financial_model: Trip contributions are handled through an external crowdfunding/payment provider; Crewly tracks contribution context and references only. Private event balances are tracking-only and remain separate.
validated_product_model.receipt_model: Original receipt values are source of truth; OCR, translation, and FX conversion are display and workflow aids.

resolved_unknowns.description: Payment processor, compliance, refund, and KYC fit for organizer-controlled pooled trip support is resolved for MVP by external-provider delegation.
resolved_unknowns.next_action: Stage 3 should model a provider-agnostic external contribution boundary; Stage 5 should select the specific provider before implementation.

risk_registry.risk: OCR extraction quality may be inconsistent across international receipts.
risk_registry.mitigation: Keep manual correction in the core flow and preserve source receipt values.

risk_registry.risk: Users may not trust organizer-controlled pooled funds without strong transparency cues.
risk_registry.mitigation: Validate contribution page trust cues, ledger transparency, payout explanation, and update cadence.

risk_registry.risk: Trip contributions and organizer payouts may trigger processor, refund, KYC, compliance, or restricted business requirements if Crewly owns money movement.
risk_registry.mitigation: MVP delegates checkout, KYC, refunds, disputes, and organizer payout to an external crowdfunding/payment provider. Crewly stores contribution references/status only and does not process, hold, refund, dispute, or pay out funds directly.

risk_registry.risk: Users may confuse trip-wide pooled funds with private event payment tracking.
risk_registry.mitigation: Preserve separated ledgers, labels, summaries, authorization scopes, and audit trails.

risk_registry.risk: Public trip pages, photos, and updates may leak private information or require moderation intervention.
risk_registry.mitigation: Default to private content, require organizer promotion for public highlights, and include moderation/report flows.

risk_registry.risk: Translated receipt text or display-only currency conversion may be mistaken for authoritative financial truth.
risk_registry.mitigation: Label localized values clearly and keep original data visible in the receipt record.

recommendations: Proceed to Stage 3 with an ExternalContributionProvider boundary instead of Crewly-owned pooled payments.
Do not build direct payment processing, fund custody, refunds, disputes, or organizer payouts inside Crewly for MVP.
Select and validate the specific external provider before Stage 5 implementation planning.
Run public contribution trust testing and organizer willingness-to-pay validation before Stage 5 implementation planning.

answered_questions.question: What payment processor pattern should Crewly validate first for organizer-controlled pooled trip support?
answered_questions.answer: Option 3: use an external crowdfunding/payment provider for public contribution checkout and organizer payout; Crewly tracks contribution context only.
answered_questions.why_it_matters: This removes Crewly-owned money movement from MVP and converts payment compliance into an external-provider integration constraint.

assumptions_made: Stage 2 assumes the initial market remains consumer/social group trips as defined in Stage 1.
Stage 2 assumes private event payments remain tracking-only in MVP.
Stage 2 assumes external market research is limited to public official/product documentation and not primary user interviews.
Stage 2 assumes Crewly will not process, hold, refund, dispute, or pay out public contribution funds directly in MVP.
user_confirmations: Use Option 3: external crowdfunding/payment provider for public contributions and organizer payout.
validation_confidence_gaps: Public supporter contribution intent for non-emergency social trips.
Organizer willingness to pay and payment fee tolerance.

architecture_readiness.reason: The critical payment/compliance blocker is mitigated by external-provider delegation. Remaining low-confidence areas are downstream validation and implementation constraints, not Stage 3 blockers.

validated_product_model.public_surface: selected trip page with external-provider contribution CTA
validated_product_model.receipt_model: source values plus localized display values

operational_constraints: Separate trip and private event ledgers.
Default media and detailed updates to private.
Audit payment, ledger, receipt, visibility, and event changes.
business_constraints: Do not process, hold, refund, dispute, or pay out contribution funds directly in MVP.
scalability_constraints: Model OCR, translation, exchange-rate, media storage, moderation, and payment support costs.

completion_status.reason: Stage 2 can hand off safely because Crewly-owned pooled payments are removed from MVP and public contribution money movement is delegated to an external provider.
next_actions: Model external contribution provider boundary in Stage 3.
Keep trip and event ledgers separate.
Carry provider selection and API/export validation into Stage 5.

completion_status.reason: Stage 2 produced the required validation artifacts and resolved the critical payment/compliance blocker by adopting external-provider contribution handling for MVP.
next_actions: Model ExternalContributionProvider as a replaceable boundary.
Preserve contribution reference tracking and audit logs without Crewly-owned funds flow.
